proof_trace: `do_add_delta` calls `do_item_get` at L3265 which calls `refcount_incr`, acquiring a reference that must be released via `do_item_remove` on every non-NULL path. The early-exit path at L3271-3272 (`it->nbytes <= 2 → return NON_NUMERIC`) is the sole path missing this release; every other non-NULL return path (CAS mismatch L3276, strtoull fail L3283, alloc fail L3324, else branch L3341, success L3348) correctly calls `do_item_remove(it)`. The `refcount` field is `unsigned short` (16-bit), so 65535 leaked increments wrap it to 0, enabling a subsequent use-after-free or permanent item pinning that prevents LRU eviction.
attack_scenario: Attacker stores a key with a 1-2 byte value (e.g., `set k 0 0 2\r\n\r\n`) then sends repeated `incr k 1` (or binary INCR) commands; each call leaks one refcount unit, permanently pinning the item so it cannot be evicted and exhausting memcached memory (DoS), or wrapping the 16-bit refcount to 0 after 65535 requests to enable use-after-free.

proof_trace: do_item_get (items.c:666) increments it->refcount before returning the item pointer; every return path from do_add_delta calls do_item_remove(it) to balance this increment except the early-return at L3271-3273 when it->nbytes <= 2, which returns NON_NUMERIC with no corresponding do_item_remove call. This permanently inflates the item's refcount, preventing eviction and leaking the slab allocation.
attack_scenario: An attacker issues 'set key 0 0 0' (empty value, nbytes==2) then repeatedly sends 'incr key 1'; each incr call traverses the nbytes<=2 path in do_add_delta, incrementing the refcount by 1 without decrement, causing unbounded memory growth and eventual OOM (DoS).

proof_trace: do_item_get (line 3265) increments the item refcount; every other early-return path (CAS mismatch L3276, non-numeric L3283) calls do_item_remove(it) before returning, but the 'nbytes <= 2' guard at L3271–3273 returns NON_NUMERIC without calling do_item_remove(it), permanently leaking one refcount unit per call. The leaked refcount prevents item_free from ever being invoked, causing unbounded memory retention.
attack_scenario: An attacker stores a key with a minimal 2-byte value ('\r\n'), then sends repeated incr/decr commands via the memcached protocol; each command triggers do_item_get (refcount++) followed by the nbytes<=2 early return (no do_item_remove), leaking one refcount per request and causing gradual memory exhaustion.
